food_name: Mee Goreng Mamak (Indian Muslim Fried Yellow Noodles)
aliases: Mee Goreng Mamak (mee goreng kuning, Indian Muslim fried noodles)
Mamak Mee Goreng (Tamil Muslim hawker fried noodles)

malay: Mee Goreng Mamak (mee goreng kuning, Indian Muslim fried noodles)
tamil: Mamak Mee Goreng (Tamil Muslim hawker fried noodles)

food_subgroup: Wok-fried fresh yellow egg noodles with potato cubes, tofu, egg, bean sprouts, and curry-tomato-chili sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for mee goreng mamak specifically. Closest parent concept: stir-fried wheat noodle dish with spices and mixed vegetables (FOODON:03315595 — noodle dish). No exact match available as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for mee goreng mamak. Closest parent category: mixed dishes, noodles, pasta (A0012). Code assigned to closest parent to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mee goreng mamak or equivalent stir-fried noodle dish in clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of mee goreng mamak as served at a Malaysian or Singaporean mamak stall, approximately 320g comprising roughly 150g yellow noodles, 60g potato cubes, 40g tofu, 50g egg, and the remainder bean sprouts, tomato, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng Mamak — Institute for Medical Research Malaysia MyFCD (mee goreng mamak entry), cross-validated with Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) — Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD mee goreng mamak entry (exact dish match). Cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo for sodium and energy consistency. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched dataset at confidence 0.9 where MyFCD did not report these fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous foods: yellow egg noodles (GI ~60, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled potato cubes (GI ~78, University of Sydney GI Database); combined weighted estimate for mee goreng mamak with mixed protein and fat components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mee goreng mamak as a composite dish. GI ~68 estimated by weighted combination of yellow egg noodles (GI ~60) and potato cubes (GI ~78) per University of Sydney GI Database entries, moderated by the presence of protein (egg, tofu) and fat (palm oil) which slow gastric emptying. GL per 320g serving = (68 × 70.4g carb) / 100 = 47.9. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1,536mg per 320g plate; ~480mg per 100g) from dark soy sauce, tomato sauce, and added salt in the stir-fry sauce
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit mee goreng mamak to no more than once per week, request 'kurang masin' (less salty) and reduced dark soy sauce, which can lower sodium by approximately 400mg per serving, though the dish remains high-sodium even with modifications.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~47.9 per 320g plate; GI ~68) driven by refined yellow wheat noodles and potato cubes; high carbohydrate content of 70.4g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request 'tanpa ubi' (no potato) to reduce GL from ~48 to ~38, consider a half-portion of noodles (~160g plate), and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose excursion; late-night mamak consumption amplifies circadian insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1,536mg/serving), saturated fat (~5.8g/serving from palm oil frying), and cholesterol (~166mg/serving from egg and tofu) contribute to cardiovascular risk when consumed frequently
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit mee goreng mamak to occasional consumption (no more than once per week), request less oil and sauce, and note that the dish's saturated fat from palm oil frying and high sodium load are the primary cardiovascular concerns rather than trans fat, which is negligible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycemic load may contribute to postprandial inflammation; curry powder provides dietary curcumin (~0.5–1g per plate) below therapeutic anti-inflammatory dose but contributes to cumulative dietary intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume mee goreng mamak in moderation; the turmeric in curry powder provides sub-therapeutic curcumin, and the high GL may transiently elevate inflammatory markers, so portion control and reduced frequency are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng Mamak is an iconic Malaysian and Singaporean street food dish prepared at 'Mamak' stalls — Indian Muslim (Tamil Muslim) hawker eateries ubiquitous throughout both countries. The dish features fresh yellow wheat egg noodles stir-fried at high heat in a wok with a distinctive sauce combining dark soy sauce, tomato sauce, and chili paste, seasoned with curry powder and turmeric. Standard ingredients include firm tofu, boiled potato cubes, a cracked egg, bean sprouts, and tomato, with the dish typically served with a wedge of calamansi lime and fresh cucumber slices. The texture is soft and slightly chewy from the noodles, with crispy tofu edges and a rich, spiced, slightly sweet-savory sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 320g plate provides approximately 496 kcal, 18.6g protein, 16.6g fat, and 70.4g carbohydrate. Sodium is very high at approximately 1,536mg per plate. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium) with a very high glycemic load of ~47.9 per serving, driven by refined noodles and potato cubes. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Clinically, it is high-concern for hypertension (very high sodium), high-concern for Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL), and moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from palm oil, high sodium). Patients may request 'kurang masin' (less salty) or 'tanpa ubi' (no potato) to reduce sodium and GL respectively. The dish is a fusion of Indian spicing, Chinese noodle technique, and Malay ingredients, representing the unique culinary heritage of the Tamil Muslim community in Malaysia and Singapore. Source: IMR Malaysia MyFCD cross-validated with HPB Singapore Foodo.

confidence_reason: Exact dish match found in IMR Malaysia MyFCD (mee goreng mamak entry) with cross-validation against HPB Singapore Foodo. Core macros and key micronutrients sourced from verified primary regional databases. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched dataset at confidence 0.9. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods (yellow egg noodles, potato) with documented methodology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for mee goreng mamak not reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in primary sources for this composite dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per MyFCD; palm oil used for frying contains negligible industrial trans fats). Copper (0.12mg/100g), manganese (0.2mg/100g), and vitamin K (8.0mcg/100g) sourced from enriched dataset at confidence 0.9 as these micronutrients were not individually reported in MyFCD primary entry. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow egg noodles (~60) and boiled potato (~78), weighted by ingredient proportion and moderated by protein and fat content; no direct GI measurement for mee goreng mamak as a composite dish exists in the published literature. GL per 320g serving calculated as (68 × 70.4) / 100 = 47.9. Portion size of 320g is based on project_veritas field measurement (noodles 150g + potato 60g + tofu 40g + egg 50g + bean sprouts and sauce ~20g). The dish is a Malaysian-Singaporean fusion dish; multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per G6 Singaporean dish requirements.
